author: @bob_the_bomb4508
text:
There was a day when you could do these sort of things in chemistry class!

On a professional note though, one important safety point. In the production of fuse material ( to make a ‘fuze’) one important thing to get right is a constant burning rate. Badly made fuse will sometimes ‘skip’ across patches of more combustible mix. It can also appear to go out, in which time a considerable ‘soak’ time should be applied. 

As much as I enjoyed this I do think you should genuinely prefaced this with “don’t try this at home kids” :)

author: @tanguasimtu5823
text:
Long ago...( my high school years)
There was a hobby shop locally that sold various chemicals... the amazing thing was that they would show you what you could do with them ...
Yep ... one time burning a strip of magnesium... but your video reminded me of the reaction between potassium permanganate and glycerin... when the two were mixed .. after a few minutes the mixture would 
heat up to the point of combustion...
This was preceded by lots of smoke...
The flame was not that large...so I added potassium perchlorate to the glycerin first... I added the posssium permanganate mixied (in a small metal bottle cap) and stood back...
My father walked in just as a 6 inch flame burst from the cap... 
He was not happy...  I am now retired and still consider this to be a fond memory of my youth

They can also be used as a light filter to take IR pictures.
=edit= that said I dunno if people have floppy disks laying around anymore =\ and after looking it up, being mostly transparent in IR means it was probably quite dangerous afterall but ho hum... if i didn't have it id have stared right at the sun so ever little helps ;)

author: @galenbean3606
text: Here is a simple idea for you.  Recharge Alkaline batteries @ 1-3 ma. at 50 cents if you recharge it once you've saved .  I've recharged some using using old solar led lights that originally came with NiCd AAA batteries from the 99 cent store. I simply cut the LED to keep it from discharging the battery and leave it out for a week or so.  I know they weren't  intended to be recharged, but seem to perform as new for the first few charges.  It has cut my battery purchases  to 1/3.  ( my Roku Remote eats batteries as it uses an rf link and rechargeable NiMh batteries don't last very long or supply enough voltage, or a combination of the two. )  I enjoy your videos and when I first learned electronics they taught me tubes. ( or valves as you call them).  I usually recharge 2 at a time using an old cellphone charger and a 1k resistor until they reach 1.55-1.6v

author: @justinw1765
text:
Wait, a fuse?  What are we going to blow up?  
   Thankfully I don't need to blow up anything.  Though thermite is interesting stuff.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
I will say that I have thought about using thermite as a low weight, winter backpacking heating source.  The idea was to line my titanium cook pot with some of that Starlite approximation stuff (which holds up to thermite pretty well it seems), put some thermite in there with some DRY rocks or sand, have a small hole drilled in the pot lid to let pressure escape, and voila--warm tent.  
  A bit dangerous, but less so than most would think, and the tents I use don't usually have a floor.

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
That was awesome mate. I didn't read the title properly, just woke up and watching you with my morning coffee and for some reason I thought you made a ceramic fuse for an electronic circuit. lol, 
Its amazing how a couple of ingredients can make something this cool. You could sell the fuses onle in countries that have no fireworks restrictions. Do the fuses deteriorate in certain conditions?
To those who give thumbs down, you need your head examined, the knowledge passed on by this channel sparks many cool ideas, you need to watch when you're prepared to learn principals that can be converted into gamechangers for your own projects.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
I have seen people using thermite to create high precision iron/steel alloys [typically for experimental purposes only], I wonder if it is possible to conduct the burn inside of an enclosed box [like fire brick or a charcoal lined graphite crucible (the charcoal to both absorb spare oxygen from the environment AND to protect the crucible from breaking)] to produce magnetic metals of specific alloys for very strong magnets, preferably causing there to be a powerful magnetic field beneath the burning mixture so as to ensure that the alloy of iron and other metals to cool while in the influence of a powerful polarizing magnetic field, thereby [hopefully] forming a very powerful custom magnet for such uses as generators or motors. ~( 'w')/
If one used some cobalt, or nickel, or rare earths, perhaps they could produce a crude pressed graphite block the rough shape of a motor field or armature [depending on which they wanted to be permanently magnetized in the final product] and somewhere in the vicinity have electromagnetic coils placed inside the insulating area of the graphite, in such a manner so that the molten metal can drain down from a separate reaction into the mold where it would then cool in the presence of the magnetic field but at the same time shielding the coils from so much intensive heat.
Then one could machine the blank down to its final tolerances and compare its performance to either totally un-magnetized shapes, cold-magnetized steel, and even shaped arrays employing pre-made sintered super magnets glued into motor [or armature] frames made of plain steel.
There might be absolutely no advantage to this whatsoever, but then there just might be, one never knows.
Admittedly, machining an already magnetic material might be kinda messy, seeing as the chips would stick to the tooling a bit, but if one simply then holds a torch over the chips they should [at least momentarily] lose their magnetic properties and fall off into the bin, keeping the cutting areas [relatively] clean during the milling process.
Also, one could pull away magnetic chip to some extent with a magnet on a handle [used often to puck up dropped screws from where they have fallen in behind and inside of things] as the process proceeded, if it were not simply a CNC process where one simply trusts the machine to know what it is doing and let it do all the cutting then come back to clean off the chip later.
While this may never become any kind of revolutionary new method of mass producing motor or generator parts, it could be fairly helpful to the hobbyist experimenting with these things who could not afford to contact a large company to make such parts for them.

replies.author: @blueredbrick
replies.text:
I dont mind, at least "they" have further refined the subset of mostly responsible people interested in practical science and energy storage (what this an example of).

Just for fun I tried isolating the tantalum from tantalum capacitors.
Me naively tinking "mm, tantalum is quite inert, maybe just heating them to get rid of the rest that is not tantalum is worth a try"

well. no. :( and :)
 
the manganeseoxide that also is part of the capacitor is a similar oxidizer (when hot) to the mentioned permanganate in the video. When heated with a flame; a smooth nice glowing reaction takes place, but helas, it was not the way to get my nice tiny tantatalum sintered beads.

It was amusing tough. 
No shortcuts were presented on a silver platter that time, did not expect it either.

Afterwards i liked both tantalum and manganese and the various oxides' conductivies and how the caps are made even more. Oldfashioned maybe ? but works of art.

author: @Killianwsh
text: Very cool video Rob.  Thanks for the alternative recipe. Looks like they burn very gently, but consistently!  Curious as to how well they handle movement/wind as well as moisture.   I taught my nephews how to make fuses for the fourth of July fireworks with cotton string (rag mop head actually :) and a simple mixture of Potassium Nitrate  & table sugar.  They really worked great in the wind resistance dept. and were very resistant to snuffing out , so long as they were kept VERY dry.  Even leaving them in the baking pan on the counter over night exposed to the humidity in the air was enough to make em hard to keep lit until we baked them again.
